title: Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate
subtitle: The reported annual death rate from [infectious](#dod:communicable-diseases) and [parasitic diseases](#dod:parasitic-infection) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.
caption:
Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate, 1950 to 2023
The reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people, based on the underlying cause listed on death certificates.

Across the period from 1950 to 2023, the reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people shows distinct trajectories for the six nations tracked. The vertical axis measures mortality rates ranging from 0 to 400 deaths per 100,000 population. South Africa presents a unique and extreme pattern compared to the other countries, with data beginning in the mid-1990s at roughly 125 deaths per 100,000. The line for South Africa rises steeply to a peak just under 400 around 2006, before falling rapidly to approximately 130 by 2023. In contrast, the United States begins the timeline in 1950 with the highest rate among the group at roughly 80 deaths per 100,000, followed by a steady decline to below 20 by the 1970s, where it remains relatively flat through 2023. France, Germany, and Australia all start near 40 deaths per 100,000 in 1950 and follow a similar downward path, stabilizing near the bottom of the chart below 10 deaths per 100,000 for the majority of the observed period. Russia and Brazil enter the dataset later, around 1980, with Brazil starting near 65 and Russia near 25. Both lines show a general downward trend over subsequent decades, with Brazil finishing near 30 and Russia near 25 in 2023. The visual gap between the South African data series and the cluster of other nations remains the most prominent feature of the chart throughout the late 20th and early 21st centuries.
